message.reasoning_content:
We need to create second library that depends on first library and calls its public function.

Project structure: library-chain/feature (project). So we need to create a new module under library-chain/feature that imports example.com/core and calls core.Hello.

We need go.mod for feature module, maybe module example.com/feature.

Add a file feature.go with a function that calls core.Hello? Or a main program? The stage goal: "Create a second library that depends on the first library and calls its public function." So a library (not a main) that imports core and provides a function that calls core.Hello. Could also have a sample usage? But validators: feature-references-core: failure_class=api_contract, feature-builds: failure_class=build. So they will check that feature library references core correctly (API contract). So we need to expose a function that calls core.Hello.

Thus create library-chain/feature/go.mod, library-chain/feature/feature.go.

In go.mod: module example.com/feature, require example.com/core v0.0.0? Since core is local, we can use replace directive. Use go.mod with replace example.com/core => ../core. Also require example.com/core v0.0.0.

So go.mod content:

module example.com/feature

go 1.22

require example.com/core v0.0.0

replace example.com/core => ./../core

But relative path from feature to core: "../core". Since go.mod is in library-chain/feature, relative path is "../core". So replace example.com/core => ../core.

Add feature.go:

package feature

import "example.com/core"

func Greet(name string) string {
    return core.Hello(name)
}

Optionally add a comment.

That's all.

We need to include any other files? Possibly a test? Not required.

Thus JSON with two files.

Make sure path uses forward slashes.

Return JSON.
